John's, we miss you so much.<|15.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What I miss more than anything else is being here with you and hearing your stories, your<|7.44|> <|7.44|> own And as you tell these<|18.84|> <|18.84|> stories in your own way, and as you sort through your own memories,<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Above all, become aware that God is with you.<|6.42|> <|6.42|> God is listening. God is very close and oh so curious about how your story is going to turn out<|15.76|> <|15.76|> and what you're going to discover as you sort through your own memories.<|20.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.80|> The 23rd Psalm, our psalm for this service, is about the Lord being very close as we make our journey.<|10.58|> <|11.86|> The Lord is my shepherd, of course, and that wonderful image.<|16.42|> <|17.52|> Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, thou art with me.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.78|> Storytelling becomes a form of prayer when we realize and imagine that God is listening,<|9.76|> <|10.60|> that God is close, that God is curious,<|13.10|> <|13.10|> that God, in other words, is sitting on the edge of her seat just waiting to hear what we have to say.<|22.92|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>

segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.84|> - A couple of weeks ago, Bert Womack died.<|4.72|>

<|12.20|> of this cathedral in 2000 and 2001.<|15.50|>
<|16.96|> Bert is a great storyteller,<|19.28|>
<|19.28|> and in many conversations with him these last few years,<|24.28|>

<|27.38|> So that's what I remember most.<|28.90|>

<|12.90|> And so when Bert died, shortly after that, I called Joanne, his wonderful wife, who's<|18.12|>
<|18.12|> a member of our parish, and I said, well, Joanne, the stories in heaven just got a lot<|26.26|>
<|26.26|> more interesting than...<|27.60|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And Joanne in her Texas accent replied immediately,<|4.24|>
<|5.36|> and broader, and broader.<|8.24|>
<|10.86|> Oh, St. John's, we miss you so much.<|15.72|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What I miss more than anything else is being here with you and hearing your stories, your<|7.44|>
<|7.44|> own accents, your own facial expressions as you talk about what's going on in your life.<|14.00|>
<|14.00|> Every Sunday, on normal Sundays at this very cathedral, thousands of stories are told and<|21.48|>
<|21.48|> all of them are holy.<|23.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> There's an old saying that God made people because God loves stories.<|5.38|>
<|6.92|> So I encourage you in this unusual season to keep telling your stories.<|12.78|>
<|12.94|> Get your stories out.<|14.24|>
<|15.46|> We're at our most human when we're the most loving and when we're telling our stories.<|21.08|>
<|21.28|> So get them out. Get them out in any way you can.<|24.30|>
<|24.48|> Email them. Write them on the phone calls with people.<|28.00|>

<|4.86|> And if you're alone, tell your story to yourself, which inevitably involves<|11.22|>

<|18.84|> stories in your own way, and as you sort through your own memories,<|22.70|>

<|11.86|> The Lord is my shepherd, of course, and that wonderful image.<|16.42|>
<|17.52|> Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, thou art with me.<|23.40|>
